title: Recursion: Tower of Hanoi & Ackermann Function

content.value: A recursive function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller version of the same problem, until it reaches a base case that can be answered directly. Every recursive function needs: (1) a base case to stop, and (2) a recursive case that moves toward the base case.

Classic recursion example: move n disks from a source peg to a destination peg, using an auxiliary peg, never placing a larger disk on a smaller one. The insight: to move n disks, first move the top n-1 disks out of the way, move the largest disk directly, then move the n-1 disks onto it.
Tower of Hanoi requires 2^n - 1 moves for n disks, which is why recursion is so natural here: trying to write it iteratively is much harder than the recursive version.

A function used to illustrate recursion that grows extremely fast and is NOT primitive recursive (it can't be written as a simple bounded loop). It's a textbook example of how deep, mutually-referencing recursion can compute things that simple iteration cannot easily express.
Ackermann(4, n) is already astronomically large - never test it with m or n above 3 in a live demo, it will exhaust the call stack.

Recursion often makes code shorter and closer to the mathematical definition of a problem, but each call consumes stack memory. Deeply recursive functions without a clear base case (or with one that's reached too slowly) cause stack overflow.

quiz.question: Every recursive function must have:
options: A base case and a recursive case

quiz.question: How many moves does the Tower of Hanoi require for n disks?
Why is the Ackermann function notable in computer science?
options: It grows extremely fast and is not expressible as simple bounded iteration
